utterances: Thanks very much, Irma.
You're welcome.
Now, let's - go, I'm sorry.
Well, Neal, I was just going to say that I think, to a great degree, a lot of it has to do with authenticity.
And one of the things that fashion, sort of, helps to illustrate is a certain degree of authenticity.
I mean, people make choices about, you know, how they want to be perceived.
And if you're, sort of, constantly shifting from one look to a completely different look to yet another look, there - you do come across as someone who hasn't yet figured out, well, who am I really?

And when people talk about style, one of the things that they say is that women, or even men, who have had great style have been people whose appearance really reflects who they are, and they feel very comfortable maintaining that specific style over the course of many years.
They're not changing with the wind.
Let's get J.P.(ph) on the line.
J.P.
calling from Veneta, Oregon.
J.P.
(Caller): Well, hi.
Hi.
J.P.: I like this.
I wanted to point out that Hillary Clinton's moment of emotion came the day after the debate.
And John Edwards said, hey, this isn't political; this is personal.
And that's exactly what Hillary said the next day.
I am a boomer and so I know Hillary as a groundbreaker.
You know, she was one of the first to go to law school and things.
And so, I hold her to a higher standard.
Is that fair?
J.P.: No.
(Soundbite of laughter)
J.P.: No, but I do hear all of the words, not as sexist words, what people describe her as a calculating person.
Well, isn't that what we want to negotiate for our country?
So, calculating is not a bad thing.
And ambition - well, if you're not ambitious, you're not going to run for president of the United States.
Yes, Jane has some wonderful bits in her essay.
She says - you know, the question I have for Barack Obama is just, can he do the job?
The question I have for Hillary Clinton is, why does she want the job?
What kind of woman does that make her, you know?
And she goes on to say that she takes Hillary personally.
She takes her seriously, more seriously, really, than she would ever dream of taking a male candidate.
J.P.: Yes.
And I think that is because we can relate to her in so many ways.
I mean, at least us baby boomers, she reminds us of certain things in ourselves.
I think that's one of the reasons we judge her more harshly and we expect more of her.

But, you know, it's - this is why the exercise of working on and reading this book, I think, is very interesting because it helps you, kind of, hash out - it's helped me anyway - kind of hash out which of my reactions, I think, are legit and which of my reactions, I think, are sort of psycho-nonsense.
J.P.: I also want her pulled together, though.
You know, they were talking about fashion and we want to see messiness.
That may be for the young ones…
(Soundbite of laughter)
J.P.: …but we know that this is business, and we want her looking like a professional businesswoman.

But the other deal is, when she was first lady within the first little chunk that she was first lady, she got together the medical people, none of whom she was going to tell us who they were, and slammed the door.
She actually showed Dick Cheney how to - you know, to work the energy deal…
Yeah, his got through, though.
Yeah.
J.P.: Excuse me?
His got through.
J.P.: Well, he slammed the door harder than she did and everybody pointed out her error; nobody pointed out Dick's error even though they saw them.
I think they've been busy doing that ever since.
But anyway, Veneta, thanks very much for the call.
We appreciate it.
J.P.: Uh-huh.
Bye-bye.
J.P.: Bye-bye.
We're talking about a new book, ""Thirty Ways of Looking at Hillary."" You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
Here's an e-mail from Jane(ph) in Oklahoma City.
As a 58-year-old woman professional, I think I understand Hillary.
You don't get to her level, in her chosen profession, by being a mouse.
Power becomes her.
Sticking with her man when wronged shows she is savvy enough to use him to her own advantage.
My vote will be hers.
Let's see if we can get another caller in.
Rona(ph) is with us.
Rona calling from Marble Canyon in Arizona.
Hi.
Gee-whiz, you know, Ginger Rogers did everything Fred Astaire did, and she did it backwards in high heels.
I think it's really dumb of women to hold Hillary to a so-called higher standard.
How high can you go?
Well, she's done everything to the maximum.
Better than most anybody, men and women, putting everything into, you know, putting her mind to it.
And these people - we've had a number of mindless presidents most recently, with no intellectual depth.
I think that the problem is with women themselves.
What do you think, Dahlia Lithwick?
I think that that's a great point.
And I think - I guess Jane made it just before.

I think that at the core of some of the most interesting essays in this book, women have a huge problem with power, and they have a huge problem with women who seek power and who do it, sort of, brazenly and boldly.
You know, one of the things that I think is so interesting is the extent to which women are very comfortable with the narrative that goes, you know, Nancy Pelosi's story.
I was called to this job.
I - you know, I'm kicking and screaming but I had to do it.
I made a deathbed promise to someone.
Laura Bush's, you know, I still hate giving speeches.
I really would be much happier reading a book.
That's a narrative women are comfortable with.
Women who, sort of - Benazir Bhutto, you know?
Well, I just happen to fall into this.
And whoops, you know, now, I'm powerful.
That's much easier, I think, for women.
Hillary stands for something very different.
This is a woman who, you know, from her days in college just went for it.

And I think several of the callers have, sort of, made the point that that makes women very, very uncomfortable, and really, some of the most intriguing essays in the book are trying to explore what is it about just absolute bare-knuckled ambition that makes women so anxious?
On that point, here's an e-mail we got from another Jane(ph), this one in Tucson, Arizona.
I'm a dedicated Democrat and a feminist and very much against Hillary.
If elected, I fear she will be so unpopular as to set back the progress of all female politicians.
She and Bill will stop at nothing to achieve their ambition.
This they proved this week with smears against Obama.
While I'm anxious to elect our first female president, I do not believe she is the one.
And, Susan Morrison, there's an aspect of that in some of your contributions as well.
Well, it is interesting.

A handful of the women say, look, I - Lionel Shriver, for instance, says, it's an antifeminist story for the nation's first female presidential candidate to have gotten where she is on her husband's coattails, and that's just depressing.

In the introduction I quote the late Wendy Wasserstein, who was originally a huge Hillary supporter and then was completely dismayed to find that after the Lewinsky scandal, that's when Hillary's popularity really soared.
But what does it mean for our country that we can only really love and admire and back a woman when we see her as a downtrodden victim.
On the other hand, I think that - you know, hang on.
I just lost my train of though.
What was the…
(Soundbite of laughter)
Well, it was the fear that her unpopularity would make it difficult for any other woman politician to follow her.
And in that circumstance, shouldn't we leave it to the legislature to deal with it?
And I'd like to thank our guests.

Susan Morrison, who you just heard, editor of the new book, ""Thirty Ways of Looking at Hillary,"" and two of her contributors - Dahlia Lithwick, who wrote the essay, ""Can You Forgive Her?"" senior legal correspondent and senior editor at the online magazine Slate.com; and Robin Givhan, the Pulitzer Prize-winning fashion reviewer for The Washington Post.
And they joined us from our bureau in New York.
Thanks to you all very much.
Joe Palca will be here as a guest host tomorrow.
We'll see you again on Monday.
I'm Neal Conan.
It's TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
Passwords are one big gripe when it comes to technology.
Another - all our electronic devices don't necessarily talk to one another.
That's a big problem when it comes to the so-called Internet of things, or IOT.
We can buy thermostats, doorbells, refrigerators and other stuff that can connect to the Internet, but not to each other.
And they can be really expensive also.
Liz Reid of member station WESA in Pittsburgh looks at how some researchers are tackling this problem.
John Essey and his wife, Adrienne, along with their daughter, Aurelia...
Ari.
...And their labradoodle, Ozwold, live in a modest two-story home on a tree-lined street in the suburbs north of Pittsburgh.
From the outside, it looks just like any other house in the neighborhood.
But this house has a brain.
It knows we're home.
Doors unlock.
It kind of sets the mood for the rest of the house, too.
It turns on lights, sets the thermostat accordingly.
Essey is an engineer at Uber and an early adopter of the Internet of things.
He can control his lights with his Amazon Echo or an array of touchpad sensors he's installed throughout the home.
Sensors tell him when there's water in the basement or a leak under the sink.
While Essey's setup might sound a little like science fiction, it's a prototype of the future.
Gierad Laput, a Ph.D. student at Carnegie Mellon University, says as the IOT becomes more ingrained in our daily lives, there are a couple of ways people are turning ordinary homes into smart homes.
